qa_pairs.question: How do you add new files or directories to an existing uncompressed `tar` archive file in Linux?
qa_pairs.answer: You can add new files or directories to an existing plain `tar` archive using the `-r` (append) option. For example, to add `new.log.dat` to `results.tar`, you would use the command `tar -rf results.tar new.log.dat`. Similarly, to add a directory like `report/`, you would use `tar -rf results.tar report/`. This functionality is only available for uncompressed `tar` archives.

qa_pairs.question: Is it possible to add files directly to a `tar` archive that has been compressed using `gzip` (`.gz`) or `bzip2` (`.bz2`)?
qa_pairs.answer: No, files cannot be added directly to compressed `tar` archives (e.g., `*.tar.gz` or `*.tar.bz2`). Files can only be added to plain, uncompressed `tar` archives.

qa_pairs.question: What command is used to combine the contents of two separate `tar` archive files into a single `tar` archive?
qa_pairs.answer: To combine two existing `tar` archive files into one, you use the `-A` (concatenate) option. For instance, to add the contents of `report.tar` to `results.tar`, the command would be `tar -A -f results.tar report.tar`.
